meta.title: Introduction to Sets
meta.subject: math
meta.description: Learn what a set is, how to write sets using different methods, types of sets, and relationships between sets.

items.back: A set is a well-defined collection of objects. 'Well-defined' means we can clearly say whether any object belongs or does not belong to the collection. Example: {2, 4, 6, 8} is a well-defined set of even numbers less than 10.
items.hint: Can you always say YES or NO if something belongs?

items.back: ∈ means 'belongs to' or 'is a member of'. If P = {2, 3, 5, 7}, then 2 ∈ P means '2 belongs to set P'. 4 ∉ P means '4 does not belong to set P'.
items.hint: ∈ looks like 'E' — think Element

items.back: All members are written inside curly braces { }, separated by commas. Each member is written only once. Example: A = {1, 4, 9} is the set of square numbers less than 10.
items.hint: List every member inside { }, no repeats!

items.back: The common property of all members is described inside braces. Example: A = {square numbers less than 10}, B = {factors of 18}.
items.hint: Describe WHAT the members have in common

items.back: A variable and its property are written inside braces. Example: A = {x : x is a square number less than 10}. Read as: 'A is the set of all x such that x is a square number less than 10'.
items.hint: Format is {x : x has this property}

items.front: 🔢 What is the Cardinal Number of a set?
items.back: The cardinal number n(A) is the total count of elements in a set. If A = {1, 4, 9}, then n(A) = 3. An empty set has n(A) = 0.
items.hint: Count the members → that is n(A)

items.back: An empty set has no elements. It is written as { } or ∅. Example: A = {oceans in Nepal} = { } because Nepal has no oceans. n(A) = 0.
items.hint: No members at all — written as { } or ∅

items.back: A singleton or unit set has exactly one element. Example: A = {the highest peak of the world} = {Sagarmatha}, so n(A) = 1. Also, B = {prime number between 6 and 10} = {7}.
items.hint: Exactly ONE member inside

items.front: ♾️ Finite vs Infinite Set — what is the difference?
items.back: A finite set has a countable number of elements that comes to an end. Example: W = {0,1,2,...,9}, n(W) = 10. An infinite set never ends. Example: N = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, ...} — shown with '...'.
items.hint: Finite = counting stops. Infinite = never ends (look for ...)

items.front: 🔗 Equal, Equivalent, Overlapping, Disjoint — what are these?
items.back: Equal sets: same elements. A = {e,f,g,h} = B = {g,f,h,e}. Equivalent: same count, different elements. P~Q. Overlapping: share at least one element. Disjoint: share NO elements.
items.hint: Equal = same elements. Equivalent = same COUNT. Overlapping = share some. Disjoint = share none.

items.question: Which of these is a well-defined collection (a proper set)?
options: A collection of nice Nepali songs
A collection of tall students in Class 6
items.explanation: 'A collection of fruits' is well-defined because we can clearly say whether any object is a fruit. 'Tasty', 'nice', and 'tall' are subjective — different people may have different opinions.
items.hint: Can we say YES or NO for every object without doubt?

items.question: Which of these is a well-defined collection (a proper set)?
items.answer: A collection of rivers of Nepal
options: A collection of long rivers of Nepal
A collection of rivers of Nepal
items.explanation: 'A collection of rivers of Nepal' is well-defined. 'Long' and 'beautiful' are subjective, so those are not well-defined.
items.hint: Avoid collections that depend on personal opinions

items.question: If N = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5} and O = {1, 3, 5, 7, 9}, is 4 ∈ N true or false?
items.explanation: 4 is in set N = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5}, so 4 ∈ N is True.
items.hint: Check if 4 appears in N

items.question: If N = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5} and O = {1, 3, 5, 7, 9}, is 4 ∈ O true or false?
items.explanation: 4 is not in set O = {1, 3, 5, 7, 9}, so 4 ∈ O is False. We should write 4 ∉ O.
items.hint: Check if 4 appears in O

items.question: If N = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5}, is 5 ∉ N true or false?
items.explanation: 5 is in N = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5}, so the correct statement is 5 ∈ N, not 5 ∉ N. The given statement is False.
items.hint: Check if 5 is inside N

items.question: If O = {1, 3, 5, 7, 9}, is 7 ∈ O true or false?
items.explanation: 7 is in O = {1, 3, 5, 7, 9}, so 7 ∈ O is True.
items.hint: Look for 7 in set O

items.question: If N = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5}, is 6 ∈ N true or false?
items.explanation: 6 is not in N = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5}, so 6 ∈ N is False. The correct statement is 6 ∉ N.
items.hint: Does N go up to 5 or higher?

items.question: If O = {1, 3, 5, 7, 9}, is 1 ∈ O true or false?
items.explanation: 1 is in O = {1, 3, 5, 7, 9}, so 1 ∈ O is True.
items.hint: 1 is the first element of set O

items.question: If W = {0, 1, 2, 3, 4} and E = {2, 4, 6, 8}, which symbol fills the blank: 6 ___ E?
items.explanation: 6 is in E = {2, 4, 6, 8}, so 6 ∈ E (6 belongs to E).
items.hint: Is 6 one of the members of set E?

items.question: If W = {0, 1, 2, 3, 4} and E = {2, 4, 6, 8}, which symbol fills the blank: 3 ___ W?
items.hint: Is 3 listed in set W?

items.question: If W = {0, 1, 2, 3, 4} and E = {2, 4, 6, 8}, which symbol fills the blank: 5 ___ W?
items.hint: W goes from 0 to 4 — is 5 inside?

items.question: If W = {0, 1, 2, 3, 4} and E = {2, 4, 6, 8}, which symbol fills the blank: 1 ___ E?
items.explanation: 1 is not in E = {2, 4, 6, 8}, so 1 ∉ E.
items.hint: E contains only even numbers 2, 4, 6, 8

items.question: If W = {0, 1, 2, 3, 4} and E = {2, 4, 6, 8}, which symbol fills the blank: 4 ___ E?
items.hint: Is 4 listed in set E?

items.question: If W = {0, 1, 2, 3, 4}, which symbol fills the blank: 7 ___ W?
items.hint: W only goes up to 4

items.question: A = {prime numbers between 10 and 20}. Which is the correct listing of set A?
items.explanation: Prime numbers between 10 and 20: 11, 13, 17, 19. Note: 15 = 3×5 is not prime; 12, 14, 16, 18 are even and not prime.
items.hint: A prime number has exactly two factors: 1 and itself

items.question: B = {letters of the word 'elephant'}. Which is the correct listing of set B?
items.answer: {e, l, p, h, a, n, t}
options: {e, l, p, h, a, n, t}
{e, l, e, p, h, a, n, t}
items.explanation: The word 'elephant' has letters e, l, e, p, h, a, n, t. In a set, each member is listed only once, so e appears just once: {e, l, p, h, a, n, t}.
items.hint: Each letter is listed only once in a set — no repeats!

items.question: C = {x : x is an odd number less than 5}. Which is the correct listing of set C?
items.explanation: Odd numbers less than 5 are 1 and 3. (5 is not less than 5, so it is not included.)
items.hint: Odd numbers: 1, 3, 5, 7... — which are LESS THAN 5?

items.question: D = {y : y is a factor of 12}. Which is the correct listing of set D?
items.explanation: Factors of 12 are all numbers that divide 12 exactly: 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, and 12. Check: 12÷1=12, 12÷2=6, 12÷3=4, 12÷4=3, 12÷6=2, 12÷12=1.
items.hint: Which numbers divide 12 with no remainder?

items.question: How do we write '9 belongs to the set N' in set notation?
items.explanation: ∈ means 'belongs to' or 'is a member of'. So '9 belongs to the set N' is written as 9 ∈ N.
items.hint: ∈ means 'belongs to'

items.question: How do we write '3 is not an element of Z' in set notation?
items.explanation: ∉ means 'does not belong to'. So '3 is not an element of Z' is written as 3 ∉ Z.
items.hint: ∉ is ∈ crossed out — NOT a member

items.question: How do we write '7 does not belong to {2, 4, 6, 8}' in set notation?
items.hint: Is 7 an even number?

items.question: W = {the whole numbers less than 6}. Which is the correct listing?
items.explanation: Whole numbers start from 0. Whole numbers less than 6 are: 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5.
items.hint: Whole numbers start from 0, not 1

items.question: M = {the first five multiples of 4}. Which is the correct listing?
items.hint: Multiply 4 by 1, 2, 3, 4, 5

items.question: A = {letters of the word 'MATHEMATICS'}. How many elements does A have?
items.explanation: MATHEMATICS has letters: M, A, T, H, E, M, A, T, I, C, S. Removing duplicates: {M, A, T, H, E, I, C, S} = 8 unique letters.
items.hint: Remove duplicate letters — each is listed only once

items.question: P = {x : x is a prime number, x < 20}. How many elements does P have?
items.explanation: Primes less than 20: 2, 3, 5, 7, 11, 13, 17, 19 — that is 8 elements.
items.hint: List all primes: 2, 3, 5, 7... up to but not including 20

items.question: B = {y : y is a composite number, y < 10}. Which is the correct listing?
items.explanation: Composite numbers have more than 2 factors. Less than 10: 4 (=2×2), 6 (=2×3), 8 (=2×4), 9 (=3×3). Note: 1 is neither prime nor composite.
items.hint: Composite = not prime and not 1

items.question: What is the set-builder form of W = {0, 1, 2, 3, 4}?
items.answer: {x : x is a whole number, x < 5}
options: {x : x is a whole number, x < 5}
{x : x is a natural number, x < 5}
{x : x is a whole number, x ≤ 5}
{x : x is an even number, x < 5}
items.explanation: W contains whole numbers (starting from 0) less than 5, so W = {x : x is a whole number, x < 5}.
items.hint: Whole numbers include 0. What is the condition x must satisfy?

items.question: What is the set-builder form of O = {1, 3, 5, 7, 9}?
items.answer: {x : x is an odd number, x ≤ 9}
options: {x : x is an odd number, x ≤ 9}
{x : x is an even number, x ≤ 9}
{x : x is a prime number, x ≤ 9}
{x : x is a natural number, x ≤ 9}
items.explanation: The set {1, 3, 5, 7, 9} contains odd numbers up to 9, so the set-builder form is {x : x is an odd number, x ≤ 9}.
items.hint: What property do 1, 3, 5, 7, 9 all share?

items.question: What is the set-builder form of F = {1, 3, 5, 15}?
options: {x : x is an odd number, x ≤ 15}
{x : x is a prime number, x ≤ 15}
{x : x is a multiple of 3, x ≤ 15}
items.explanation: 1, 3, 5, 15 are exactly the factors of 15 (15÷1=15, 15÷3=5, 15÷5=3, 15÷15=1). So F = {x : x is a factor of 15}.
items.hint: Which numbers divide 15 exactly?

items.question: What type of set is A = {2, 4, 6, 8, 10, ...}?
items.explanation: The '...' shows the set continues without end. A set whose elements cannot be completely listed is an infinite set.
items.hint: Look for '...' — does the counting ever stop?

items.question: If N = {11, 12, 13, 14}, what is n(N)?
items.explanation: n(N) is the cardinal number — the total count of elements. N has 4 elements: 11, 12, 13, 14. So n(N) = 4.
items.hint: Count how many members are in the set

items.question: S = {1, 4, 9, 16, 25, 36, 49}. What is n(S)?
items.explanation: S contains the square numbers: 1², 2², 3², 4², 5², 6², 7² = 1, 4, 9, 16, 25, 36, 49. That is 7 elements, so n(S) = 7.
items.hint: Count the elements: 1, 4, 9, 16, 25, 36, 49

items.question: A = {e, f, g, h} and B = {g, f, h, e}. What is the relationship between sets A and B?
items.explanation: A and B contain exactly the same elements in a different order. Two sets with the same elements are called equal sets. A = B.
items.hint: Are the members exactly the same — just in different order?

items.question: X = {3, 6, 9, 12} and Y = {1, 3, 5, 7}. What is the relationship between X and Y?
items.explanation: X and Y do not have the same elements but both have 4 elements: n(X) = n(Y) = 4. Sets with the same number of elements are called equivalent sets.
items.hint: Do they have the same COUNT of elements?

items.question: P = {1, 3, 5, 7, 9} and Q = {2, 4, 6, 8}. What is the relationship between P and Q?
items.explanation: P contains odd numbers and Q contains even numbers. They share no common element, so they are disjoint sets.
items.hint: Do P and Q share any common element?

items.question: A = {5, 10, 15, 20} and B = {4, 8, 12, 16, 20}. What is the relationship between A and B?
items.explanation: A and B share the element 20, so they are overlapping sets. Two sets that have at least one element in common are overlapping.
items.hint: Is there any number that appears in both A and B?

items.question: What type of set is A = {prime number between 5 and 7}?
items.explanation: The only integer strictly between 5 and 7 is 6. But 6 = 2×3 is not prime. So A = { }, which is an empty set.
items.hint: List integers strictly between 5 and 7 — is any of them prime?

items.question: What type of set is D = {square numbers between 10 and 20}?
items.explanation: Square numbers between 10 and 20: 4²=16. Only 16 qualifies (3²=9 < 10, 5²=25 > 20). So D = {16}, which is a singleton set.
items.hint: List squares: 9, 16, 25 — which lie strictly between 10 and 20?

items.question: What type of set is B = {multiples of 2 less than 20}?
items.explanation: B = {2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, 14, 16, 18} — it has exactly 9 elements and comes to an end. So B is a finite set with n(B) = 9.
items.hint: Does the list of multiples of 2 less than 20 ever end?

items.question: What type of set is C = {multiples of 2 greater than 20}?
items.explanation: C = {22, 24, 26, 28, ...} — there is no greatest even number, so this list never ends. C is an infinite set.
items.hint: Do multiples of 2 greater than 20 ever stop?

items.question: If D = {x : x is a square number, x < 75}, what is n(D)?
items.explanation: Square numbers less than 75: 1, 4, 9, 16, 25, 36, 49, 64 (since 8²=64 < 75, but 9²=81 ≥ 75). So n(D) = 8.
items.hint: List 1², 2², 3²... and stop when the result reaches or passes 75

items.question: A = {Natural numbers less than 10}. What is n(A)?
items.explanation: Natural numbers less than 10: {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9} — that is 9 elements. Note: 0 is not a natural number.
items.hint: Natural numbers start at 1 — count up to but not including 10

items.question: B = {factors of 18}. What is n(B)?
items.hint: Which numbers divide 18 exactly? Count them.

items.question: C = {letters of the word 'apple'}. What is n(C)?
items.explanation: The word 'apple' has letters a, p, p, l, e. Removing duplicate p: C = {a, p, l, e}, so n(C) = 4.
items.hint: Remove repeated letters — each letter is listed only once

items.question: A = {x : x is a natural number less than 1}. What type of set is A?
items.explanation: The smallest natural number is 1. No natural number is less than 1. So A = { }, an empty set with n(A) = 0.
items.hint: The smallest natural number is 1 — can any be less than 1?

items.question: P = {y : y is a prime number between 12 and 15}. What is n(P)?
items.explanation: Integers strictly between 12 and 15: 13 and 14. 13 is prime; 14 = 2×7 is not. So P = {13} and n(P) = 1. It is a singleton set.
items.hint: Check 13 and 14 — which one is prime?

items.question: A = {whole numbers less than 5} and B = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5}. What is their relationship?
items.explanation: A = {0, 1, 2, 3, 4} (whole numbers include 0) and B = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5}. Both have n = 5 elements but different members. They are equivalent sets (A ~ B), not equal.
items.hint: Remember: whole numbers start from 0. Are the elements exactly the same?

items.question: P = {x : x is a prime number, x < 10} and Q = {7, 5, 3, 2}. What is their relationship?
items.explanation: P = {2, 3, 5, 7} and Q = {7, 5, 3, 2}. Both sets contain exactly the same elements. So P = Q (equal sets).
items.hint: List P in order and compare element by element with Q

items.question: A = {factors of 12} and B = {factors of 18}. Are A and B overlapping or disjoint?
items.explanation: A = {1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 12} and B = {1, 2, 3, 6, 9, 18}. Common elements: {1, 2, 3, 6}. Since they share elements, they are overlapping sets.
items.hint: Find factors of both 12 and 18 — do any appear in both lists?

items.question: P = {first five multiples of 4} and Q = {first five multiples of 7}. Are they overlapping or disjoint?
items.explanation: P = {4, 8, 12, 16, 20} and Q = {7, 14, 21, 28, 35}. No common elements, so they are disjoint sets.
items.hint: List P and Q — does any number appear in both?

items.question: The letters of the word 'rhododendron' are listed correctly as...
options: {r, h, o, d, o, d, e, n, d, r, o, n}
items.explanation: The word has letters r, h, o, d, o, d, e, n, d, r, o, n. In a set, each member is written only once: {r, h, o, d, e, n} = 6 elements.
items.hint: Remember: no repeated elements in a set!

items.question: From the set N = {1, 2, ..., 10}, which is true about {3, 6, 9, 12} __ N?
items.explanation: 12 is not in N = {1, 2, ..., 10}, so {3, 6, 9, 12} is NOT a subset of N. We write {3, 6, 9, 12} ⊄ N.
items.hint: Is every element of {3, 6, 9, 12} also in N = {1 to 10}?

items.question: If A = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9} and B = {2, 3, 5, 7}, what is the set of common elements?
items.explanation: All elements of B = {2, 3, 5, 7} are also in A. So the common elements are {2, 3, 5, 7}.
items.hint: Which elements appear in both A and B?

items.question: If P = {2, 4, 6, 8, 10} and Q = {4, 8, 12, 16}, what is the set of common elements?
items.explanation: Elements in both P and Q: 4 ∈ P and 4 ∈ Q ✓; 8 ∈ P and 8 ∈ Q ✓. So common elements = {4, 8}.
items.hint: Check each element of Q — is it also in P?

items.question: B = {letters of the word 'ball'} and C = {letters of the word 'call'}. What is their relationship?
items.explanation: B = {b, a, l} and C = {c, a, l}. n(B) = n(C) = 3, but the elements are different (b ≠ c). They are equivalent sets.
items.hint: List both sets removing duplicates — same count, same elements, or different?

items.question: P = {x : x is a prime number less than 10} and Q = {y : y is an even number less than 10}. Are P and Q overlapping or disjoint?
items.explanation: P = {2, 3, 5, 7} and Q = {2, 4, 6, 8}. The element 2 is in both P and Q, so they are overlapping sets.
items.hint: Is there any number that is both prime and even less than 10?

items.question: P = {x : x is a prime, x < 10} and Q = {y : y is even, y < 10}. What is the set R of their common elements, and what is n(R)?
items.explanation: P = {2, 3, 5, 7} and Q = {2, 4, 6, 8}. Only 2 appears in both. So R = {2} and n(R) = 1.
items.hint: 2 is the only even prime number
